title: Emily (LeGrand) Larson -Operations Interview - Bridget | Client Operations Manager - Agency
meeting_title: Emily (LeGrand) Larson -Operations Interview - Bridget | Client Operations Manager - Agency

transcript.text: Hey, Bridget.
Oh, hi, Emily.
One second.
computer just went crazy.
You are all good.
Okay.
Now I can see you.
Okay.
Perfect.
Nice to meet you.
Sorry I'm late.
Oh, no.
It's totally okay.
How's your day going?
Good.
I just got to San Francisco this morning.
I flew in.
So that's why I need to push the call back.
My flight was a bit late.
No, I totally get that.
Do you travel down to San Francisco often?
No, only, well, every three months pretty much is what I've done this year.
Okay.
That's not bad.
That's kind of nice to switch it up a little bit.
Yeah, it's not bad.
I just come from our board meetings that are quarterly and in person.
I spend the whole week so I can see people.
Where are you based?
I'm based outside of Boston.
I live in Beverly now.
Oh, cool.
Nice.
I used to live in Boston.
Yeah, I saw that.
You spent some time.
was going to see if you've been back at all since graduation or anything like that.
Just for reunions.
Like Harvard's really big on the reunions.
They go to town.
Oh, yeah.
I've seen them from a distance.
Yeah, yeah.
They're like unmissable.
So I had my tenure a couple of years ago.
I did five years.
So yeah, I'll keep going to them for sure.
They're awesome.
Awesome.
Well, it's very nice to meet you.
was just looking at your LinkedIn.
Sorry, I'm it up again.
No, you were totally okay.
Okay, cool.
Okay, yes.
Sorry.
I had already looked at it yesterday and then now I'm just remembering.
So you're at Accenture for, did a long haul at Accenture.
I, yes.
I joined right after school, traditionally, like not knowing where I wanted to go.
So consulting was kind of the natural first step for me to explore where I wanted to land.
then I ended up doing way longer than expected.
Did you like it more than you thought?
Yeah.
Or like what was keeping you there?
I think what kept me there was getting to try new opportunities.
And the thing that kept me there the longest was I was able to help start a net new contract and program basically from the ground up, which is kind of unheard of at a center.
Like normally it's on contract growth with existing clients.
And this was a net new client.
And I was able to take on a lot of responsibility pretty early in my career.
And it pushed me in a way that I didn't think I was going to be able to have as like a consultant.
And so I kind of grew with that account.
And that was kind of like my why for sticking around for I think almost seven years now.
How is it being?
I see now you're in a – is it a startup or is it like pretty big?
So, yeah, Rapid 7 is quite large.
it's 속 is It's funny.
funny.
you.
They still do operate very much in a startup mindset, even though well over 800 million ARR a year, 11,000 customers, global services.
When I was at Accenture, I was very focused in the federal industry since I was based out of DC for a really long time.

And I knew I wanted to explore industry and what it took to deliver at a commercial capacity and a commercial pace because the federal government definitely delivers and operates at a different capacity than working with another commercial client.
So that's the kind of the why as to what drew me to Rapid7 was getting able to experience like a new industry in an intersection of like customer experience and then also operations.
Program management, which are two of the things I focused on while I was at Accenture.
Yeah, I could definitely see overlap.
What does the team look like today at Rapid7?
Yep.
So I sit within the CS operations team.
I report to a senior VP who's the head of operations within that.
We have RevOps, the customer success program managers, which is myself, as well as two others.
This is a net new role that I came into in April.
So they identified a need of needing to have these new PMs kind of execute these more strategic initiatives, customer success initiatives that the VP wasn't able to take on.
kind of sounded really similar to Andy's why behind the role.
And then I also interact a lot with our data analytics team, which sits within CS.
Since we have our data folks, like primarily focus on each subset of the organization.
Okay, nice.
So what's, just to like understand the split more, like what would be, let me think how to ask this, but I'm just going pull up the questions I was going to ask as well.
Um, what like, what is like one of these initiatives you've led that is very reliant on the data team to execute?
Yes.
Um, so one of my primary, um, focus areas is around our, um, a couple of two parts, um, customer health metrics.
Um, I work hand in hand with our data analytics team and our product team, as well as our like services team, which consists of like support and our, our more technical, um, customer success.
Best advisors and were identified when I joined, that was a primary focus area was building out this customer health score and bringing in a number of components such as product health and adoption.
Where are they in the product maturity curve?
Bringing in operations aspect, like how many open support tickets do they have?
NPS, as well as a handful of more like advisor assessment as well, where the advisor that's aligned to that account gives a yes or no, just gut feeling of the health of the relationship.
like an account manager or like who, what's an advisor?
Okay, okay.
It's the new term, CSM was what they were called last year.
Now they're advisors.
I think that they're trying to fit.
Change the role a little bit more.
And so part how I kind of fed into that strategic initiative.

was one kind of setting up the success metrics and goals of the overarching project, and then working with the data analytics team to understand, based off of those success metrics, what weighting makes sense to create that health score, since not everything is created equal with how it may or may not impact a customer relationship and their ability to renew with Rapid7.

And then from there, building out what a digestible report and dashboard looks like, not just for the data analytics team, but also for the account managers who own their own very large book of business, as well as the customer success leadership.
So we made quite a few different dashboards, depending on where you kind of fit within the organization.
And from there, now that that has been stood up, we've been tackling, now that we have these insights, what do we want to do with them?
So we've divided that.

into two separate avenues, one for at-risk customers in identifying key churn indicators and building out risk mitigation playbooks for these account managers to lean on when they've identified this risk or the customer health score dips in one way or another.

On the other side of that, when we've seen the customer health score increasingly rise, we see that as an indication of expansion opportunities to upgrade their services, grow their spend with Rapid7, so also building out sales and marketing playbooks when we see the health score trending in a higher number.
Okay.
What would you say is like your personal biggest accomplishment in that project so far?
Like what I'm getting at instead of asking a hidden question is like I hear a lot of we and I'm just very curious what you do specifically.
What you're very proud of.
Yeah.
So what I'm really proud of is, one, bringing the organization together.
I would call myself the management function of bringing in the data folks, bringing in the product folks.

These are groups that typically have been siloed and focused on their end deliverable and bringing the group together to align and rally towards like one common success criteria was something I facilitated through design thinking workshops and kind of breaking down the why behind this customer health score.
So I feel like my strength is mainly being the connector and the bridge or bridge or a word, but the bridge between teams that don't typically collaborate with one another.
And I ultimately own the weighting.
Making sure that there is leadership alignment across the health score weighting.
We have data that is reliable and historical to inform that weighting.
So that was my key deliverable that I contributed to that program.
And the weighting is like across all accounts, we value NPS at this kind of thing?
Okay.
Yeah.

As well as we broke down, the thing that took the longest was working with the product team, which are very technical and very just feature oriented in breaking down what does good look like for this product?
Like what is their minimum versus what is a champion of this yeah, like product signals.
And then being able to score if they've done this, this indicates one thing.
If they've done that and done things higher on the maturity curve, that should be a separate score.
So we did that across our entire product.
Which is around 20 active products right now.
So we did that exercise about 20 times.
Wow.
And is this what you're calling the design thinking workshop or is that different?
Yep.

That was definitely part of that and bringing in some of our voice of the customer piece as well, where we read back some of the feedback that we've gotten from the products and the features to kind of show the why behind the customer health score and all of that.
So walk me through a workshop.
Like, what are you presenting during it?
And like, what are you trying to get out of the workshop?
Yep.
For some of that, for success outcomes, we bring in always, we tend to have executive sponsors for each of these strategic initiatives.
For this one, it was the VP of customer success.
I had prepped.
Tim to come in to orient these cross-functional owners to kind of discuss the why, what have we been experiencing in the past, the kind of level set to gain buy-in.

From there, we created a, I would call it, basically, it was a hands-on activity where we laid out anything could be on the table, like what do you think is important to inform if a customer is healthy or not?
So it was mainly just like throwing everything on the table.
I like this activity the most when I'm trying to get group consensus is that then everybody has like three, three sticky notes where they can use as points.
You can throw it on all three if you think that that's the most important or if you want to kind of spread it out.
And that helps shook out from things that maybe weren't the best indicators for customer health to very high priority indicators for customer health.
And once we had those that...
Yeah.
Maybe top 10, top five indicators.
We kind of did a structured deep dive in all of those of how could we, like, what does good look like for that?
How could we use that to inform customer health?
And then from there, once we've identified customer health, what types of actions do we want to be able to take based off of what that customer health score is showing?
How does it work, like, because it's kind of a big, I mean, I've never worked in a company that size.
So how does, like, the decision-making process work?
Like, I imagine you're in a room with, like, a lot of different stakeholders at different levels and everyone's throwing in ideas.
Like, how do you just drive it to, like, a conclusion or, like, walk me through that?
And it doesn't have to be, you don't have to agree with this, that they're set up being the right way either.
Like, I'm just wondering if works and, like, how do you, how else could, how could you see it working better or something?
No, that's, that's a good question.
I think that the most helpful...
A of it is having the executive sponsor to be the one championing the idea and communicating how it directly relates to the OKRs set for each fiscal year.
And having that as kind of the driving, I don't want to say force or factor, to having a collective agreement.

And if someone isn't agreeing with the executive sponsor on the direction that they want to be moving towards, we always could take the time to kind of understand what their points are or how it would relieve or how any of their stressors would be relieved, whether it be a design tweak up front on the initiative or taking that back and putting that as part of the backlog to then enhance and iterate on as we move through the program as well.
Um, do you have anything you felt was like a personal like mistake?
During this entire project?
Mistake for that program, not necessarily, but I definitely made a mistake at the beginning of working at Rapid7 was that I was taking on a number of strategic initiatives prior to that.
And I didn't, I felt like I didn't have the correct direction and oversight from some of my leaders I reported to.
So I ended up saying yes to too many initiatives or kind of side projects that didn't ultimately align to this customer health score.
So in a way, I made a mistake by not prioritizing my capacity to that program.
And so...

Because of that, really in necessity, I stood up a strategic initiatives intake, execution, and closure process where any time an ask or any type of initiative that me or my team was taking on, we would thoroughly vet that out through an intake process, ask the right questions, assess the level of effort and impact, and bring that then to our senior VP for ultimate decision-making on if this is something that we wanted to be investing our time in, so that I wouldn't make that mistake again of having my efforts being split between a number of different initiatives that didn't ultimately drive to what her ultimate vision was for the team.
And was that, so that was because just like other stakeholders were also asking, yeah, I could see that being exciting.
we were in that new role, and I think a lot of people when we joined were really excited.
Excited to kind of have the execution hands in the mix, since a lot of folks are so focused on strategy and their bandwidth is quite limited to actually get some of these backlog projects done.
And starting, I really wanted to say yes to everything, being part of a new team, being part of a new company.
Um, so I learned, I learned that placing a little bit more like program management rigor around how I was prioritizing my time is going to make me more effective as a teammate.
Were there any, like, did it impact the health score project in the beginning?

In the sense that I wasn't able to dedicate my entire focus on that, because if I had stood up this intake process from day dot of joining, it would have been very clear that that was the number one item that our team should be tackling.
Um, so minimal schedule, like, impacts, but overarching, um, I would say nothing concrete to delay the rollout that happened, um, a couple weeks back.
What, so when you want to say, okay, you get it all figured out, you have the metrics, and now you're ready to build the dashboards.
How do you work with the data team?
Like, what's the process there and, like, getting them to prioritize it and then, like, literally, how does it work?
Like, what are the steps?
Yeah, um, so I have found it the easiest way to collaborate with the data team is building out for me what success looks like in terms of the UI or a UX of a dashboard.
Like, I want to be able to see these things, not being overly prescriptive of what tool I want to use, like Tableau, Salesforce, anything like that.
I more outline what success criteria is for me, and I always like to draw things.
I think at that.
That's an easier way to collaborate back and forth.
What do you do?
I'm just curious.
Oh, for me, I sometimes just use PowerPoint for myself with...
Consultant reflex.
Consultant reflex, yeah.

And then when we, just as a first draft, and then once they figure out like, oh, this makes sense to do this in Tableau, then I build a more thorough UI, but I feel like that's the best way to, since they are so detailed and technical focused and I'm more big picture, I think having a common ground of like requirements is a really effective way to communicate.
And from there, that's how like we start thinking about what's really reality.
They can say, well, with this data source, we only have history tracking for X amount of months.
With this data source, it would make sense to be reflecting this dashboard within Tableau.
And it's an...
Iterative process of making mutual agreement and then also balancing technical aspects and level of effort with what is the most digestible reporting.
Like, for example, working with the data team, I wanted it to be in Gainsight because I think that that's high, that has a great user experience.
And that's where our account teams normally sit.
They let me know it would be five times faster to develop it within Salesforce.

And we build these pros and consideration lists of the different ways we can construct a dashboard so that we can balance, like, level of effort for their development schedule and how much it would work usability-wise, like, people component for me as well.
And we try to meet in the middle as much as possible.
Um, um.
The one thing that I think that we're really strong at right now is being very open about where our backlogs are and what other items we're working on.
So the data team is very upfront with, well, this is what we have in our queue, this is what we have in the backlog, and this is where your project fits in that level of priority.
And I'm the same way as well.
With like our strategic initiatives, having that thorough backlog, we can communicate like dependencies and schedule like limitations.
Awesome.
How many people are on the data team?
And you said it's kind of like you have your own CS data team?
Yeah.
Yeah.
So there's a lead data, like lead, who is the program management aspect of it.
And she's also a developer herself.
So and then she has three direct reports.
a team of four.
Okay, cool.
And then what other tools?
So you mentioned
Gainsight, Salesforce, Tableau.
What else do you all use like on a regular basis?
Yep.
So we use, just like you said, Gainsight, Tableau, Salesforce.
We also use as inputs for like the customer health score, Gong.
So we use that.
We also use more for the program management side of the house.
use Jira.
And we're a Google, Google Suites company as well.
But at my time at Accenture, I've, I've experienced with all the Microsoft Suites, Microsoft Project, Miro, and some of those more like design thinking tools as well.
Cool.
Okay.
Sorry.
I feel like I've hammered you with questions.
I've also, this is the, you're the first person we've interviewed for this role.
So I'm trying to like get, no, no, of course, myself as well.
But I think I have like a good sense.
So thank you.
very much for going into detail.
What can I answer for you about the role or the company or me or anything?
Yeah.
So I noticed that your role, Bridle, is VP of Operations.
And I kind of wanted to learn a little bit more of what does that mean for you day to day?
And what parts of the company are you touching?
And where are your priority areas kind of are right now?
Yeah.
Depends on the day.
I have some of my teams.
I've got like finance, HR, recruiting, legal, IT, and then customer operations as well.
Like I work with Andy.
And then I help a lot with sales.
So it's like, I mean, we're not a huge company.
Like we have 80 people and 50 of those people are like on the services team.
So the rest of the company, it's like, I basically manage the business teams other than engineering.
And so, yeah, it depends.
I would say the two areas I spend most of my time are...
Probably finance, like just projecting out the company for next year and thinking about that.
And then, and then like customer, like I just spend a lot of time with Andy.
It's like all of our revenue comes from services.
Most of our people are on the services team.
So it's just a lot of like trying to think about capacity planning, how to be more scalable.
Like we've essentially scaled like linearly for the last six months and we need like some step functions there.
So trying to think of like, what can we change all the time to make it like more efficient, essentially.
So yeah, I would say I end up spending a lot of time there.
Just sort of the entire leadership team, honestly, because it's like all the fires and all the revenue and everything is sitting in the services org right today.
Yes.
When you first joined, I guess, what was the first like challenge or a project that you tackled yourself?
Um.
Um.
The day I joined, I got a term sheet for the Series A finance round.
then I went into, yeah, literally at like 8 a.m.
on my first day.
So we went into 30 days of due diligence and I didn't have anyone on my team.
Like I had to do, in the financials, we had been running the company out of my, our CEO's LLC.
It was a mess.
So like, that's all I did for like 30 days straight, just try to work with lawyers to figure out how to get us through the due diligence.
But anyway, it was fine in the end, got the funding.
But yeah, that was definitely, I had a very unusual first 30 days.
They were like, no training for Bridget, straight into the role.
Yeah, exactly.
And then I guess, as we're getting closer to 2026, do you have any, what are your immediate priorities heading into the new year or goals for, for the teams that you manage?
Um, definitely like thinking further ahead on the services side, like we're really caught just being reactive.
So then we haven't built efficiency because it's hard to do that when you're just like, you know, whack-a-mole kind of situation.
So like, I want us to all have more space to think further ahead and like get in the habit of that.
Um, and then we're also launching a few like products.
So it's been a very much like bifurcate the company and I'm very like, we need to plan for that.
Like it's an entirely different profile of people that are going to be using the product and opting for services.
So we'll probably need different people working accounts and some work and different engineering and stuff.
So like we're almost building like two companies at that point, like two business units.
So it's mostly like the priorities are just like trying to think, even if we could think six months ahead, like, I mean, this is a very early company.

I mean, most startups I've been in are like this where like you're lucky if you can predict like three months ahead what's going to happen and then you get there and then everything like goes out the window.
have to do it again.
So I think if we could get a little bit ahead and like especially just on hiring and getting like some critical like high leverage roles in place, that's kind of what I'm thinking about a lot.
Yeah.
Good question.
And then I noticed for like your predominant career has been within the startup culture.
What drew you to that and like what's kind of kept you there?
Oh, I question my sanity all the time.
I like I find it very exciting.
Like obviously there's great days and terrible days and it's like more weird than anything, but every single day is different.
Everything is like a blank slate.
So I work best like autonomously without.
Like, direction even.
Like, I just like figuring stuff out on my own and throwing out ideas.
So, like, if you're a very proactive person and you like raising your hand and stuff, it's, like, the perfect environment because essentially there is, like, endless things that need to be worked on.
Like, that's what the most, that's the hardest part is, like, saying no to things because there's so few people.
So I'd say that, yeah, I like having, like, you're literally just trying to make the business survive, like, every day, basically.
So I find it good.
I find it good.
I understand why, like, a lot of people, I think it overwhelms people when you are used to direction, like, someone guiding you through a lot of stuff.
There's none of that here, like, or in any startup I've been in.
There's no one has time, unfortunately, to, like, manage or babysit or help.
It's, like, a little bit of, like, just.
I trust you, like just go your best guess, just go for it, it's gonna be better.
Literally anything is better than what exists, so just go for it, you know, it's that kind of mentality.
That's why I like it.
For me, it's just like a good personality fit as well.
Yeah, I mean, the everything I've talked to like Andy about and learning like earlier in the process, it seems very energizing.
And it seems like you all are very intentional about who you're bringing on, and how you're approaching the growth.
And it just sounds really amazing.
And something that I would love to be part of.
I've, I really enjoy operating in the gray and being able to define like my own process.
policies, like kind of what I talked about before with creating the initiatives, like backlog, that's been my, I feel like something that's been really helpful for me.
But yeah, everything you just said sounds incredible.
And where I kind of want
Nice.
Yeah.
think it's like, what gives people energy is the biggest question.

And if it's like building, like I always use the word artifacts for some reason, but like essentially, like if you do very well in a startup, if you like every week you're sharing, you're like sharing something, Hey, I built this or like, Hey, I made this thing or Hey, I made this three times better.
And I literally spent one hour on it and I made it three times better.
Like it's very easy to have impact if you just go for it.
Like that's what I am.
So some say like people who hand are hand raisers by nature do very well.
Cause it's like, wow, everything.
It's like the littlest thing in like some big company, but like, you're like, wow, that's a game changer.
I love that.
Amplified impact.
Exactly.
Cause it's like, Holy crap.
Yeah.
There's, that is way better than what exists.
That's amazing.
So I would say like that just stands out with people too.
As well.
So, um,
So yeah.
Was there anything else I could like clarify either from what I had discussed with Andy or kind of what I had walked through in our call today?
No, no.
I think it's clear.
Like basically, because you really are the first person we've met.
I just like Andy and I probably need to just calibrate again together.
Like what exactly, I mean, I really defer to her.
What would give her the most like leverage in her day to day too?
I would say just to be completely transparent, like I'm not sure how she's thinking about like, we don't have a data team at all, right?
So I don't know to what the solution is there.
Like if she needs someone who can also be building the dashboards or is there another plan for that?
There might be.
So that's just me being a little bit ignorant to the exact scope of the role, which I'll find out.
But yeah, if anything, that would, that's what's on my mind.
Yeah, of course.
And I
And I do have experience with building like high-level dashboards within Salesforce, GainSight, all of that.
The more intricate of connecting the data sources, all of that, I have definitely been able to defer to just like clarity on that part.
No, no.
Even I have like in my last startup, mean, it was over 100 people and it was around for a while, but it was a data company.
So we had data engineers.
But yeah, like I would go into like a whimsical or like a, like a graphic, like a, what Figma would work too.
And I would like mock up my own dashboard, but then I couldn't build it.
I didn't know Python or anything.
So, but I have, I don't know what her plan is.
So that's just my question mark, but I will find out soon.
She's here too.
Oh, cool.
The game's all together.
Yeah, I know.
So it be fun.
So, um, well, it was nice to meet you and we'll try to meet you in the next couple of days.
Yeah, of course.
Enjoy San Francisco.

default_summary.markdown_formatted:
## Meeting Purpose

[Interview Emily LeGrand for the Client Operations Manager role.](https://fathom.video/share/_njEukei9D_JdhVufeZ2yjy9TiAsw5jD?tab=summary&timestamp=385.0)

## Key Takeaways

  - [Emily LeGrand, a CS Program Manager at Rapid7, led a major customer health score initiative, unifying siloed teams (Data, Product, Services) to define metrics and build dashboards.](https://fathom.video/share/_njEukei9D_JdhVufeZ2yjy9TiAsw5jD?tab=summary&timestamp=619.0)
  - [Her core skill is cross-functional alignment, using design thinking workshops to build consensus and an executive sponsor to drive decisions.](https://fathom.video/share/_njEukei9D_JdhVufeZ2yjy9TiAsw5jD?tab=summary&timestamp=822.0)
  - [LeGrand self-identified an early mistake of over-commitment; her solution was to create a formal "Strategic Initiatives Intake Process" to vet projects by effort and impact, ensuring focus on top priorities.](https://fathom.video/share/_njEukei9D_JdhVufeZ2yjy9TiAsw5jD?tab=summary&timestamp=1192.0)
  - [The hiring company, a 80-person startup, needs to shift from reactive "whack-a-mole" operations to proactive, scalable systems to support its services business and upcoming product launches.](https://fathom.video/share/_njEukei9D_JdhVufeZ2yjy9TiAsw5jD?tab=summary&timestamp=1846.0)

## Topics

### Company Context & Role Need

  - [Bridget McGillivray (VP of Operations) manages all business functions (Finance, HR, IT, Legal, Customer Ops) for the 80-person startup.](https://fathom.video/share/_njEukei9D_JdhVufeZ2yjy9TiAsw5jD?tab=summary&timestamp=1688.0)
  - [**Primary Focus:** Customer Operations, as the services team (50 people) generates all company revenue.](https://fathom.video/share/_njEukei9D_JdhVufeZ2yjy9TiAsw5jD?tab=summary&timestamp=1734.0)
  - [**Key Challenges:**](https://fathom.video/share/_njEukei9D_JdhVufeZ2yjy9TiAsw5jD?tab=summary&timestamp=1846.0)
      - [**Scalability:** The company has scaled linearly for 6 months and needs "step functions" in efficiency.](https://fathom.video/share/_njEukei9D_JdhVufeZ2yjy9TiAsw5jD?tab=summary&timestamp=1734.0)
      - [**Proactive Planning:** Operations are reactive ("whack-a-mole"); the goal is to think 6+ months ahead.](https://fathom.video/share/_njEukei9D_JdhVufeZ2yjy9TiAsw5jD?tab=summary&timestamp=1846.0)
      - [**Product Launch:** New products will create two distinct business units, requiring different operational models.](https://fathom.video/share/_njEukei9D_JdhVufeZ2yjy9TiAsw5jD?tab=summary&timestamp=1846.0)
  - [**Startup Culture:** Requires high autonomy and a "go for it" mentality. Small efforts yield outsized impact ("amplified impact").](https://fathom.video/share/_njEukei9D_JdhVufeZ2yjy9TiAsw5jD?tab=summary&timestamp=1944.0)

### Candidate Experience: Rapid7 Customer Health Score

  - [**Background:** 7 years at Accenture (federal focus) → Rapid7 (commercial focus).](https://fathom.video/share/_njEukei9D_JdhVufeZ2yjy9TiAsw5jD?tab=summary&timestamp=398.0)
  - [**Current Role:** CS Program Manager (new role since April) executing strategic initiatives for the VP of Operations.](https://fathom.video/share/_njEukei9D_JdhVufeZ2yjy9TiAsw5jD?tab=summary&timestamp=548.0)
  - [**Primary Project:** Building a customer health score from scratch.](https://fathom.video/share/_njEukei9D_JdhVufeZ2yjy9TiAsw5jD?tab=summary&timestamp=619.0)
      - [**Goal:** Unify siloed teams (Data, Product, Services) around a single definition of customer health.](https://fathom.video/share/_njEukei9D_JdhVufeZ2yjy9TiAsw5jD?tab=summary&timestamp=842.0)
      - [**Metrics:** Product adoption, support tickets, NPS, and CSM ("Advisor") sentiment.](https://fathom.video/share/_njEukei9D_JdhVufeZ2yjy9TiAsw5jD?tab=summary&timestamp=634.0)
      - [**Outcome:** Dashboards for CSMs and leadership to identify at-risk customers (for churn mitigation) and expansion opportunities.](https://fathom.video/share/_njEukei9D_JdhVufeZ2yjy9TiAsw5jD?tab=summary&timestamp=769.0)
  - [**Key Contributions:**](https://fathom.video/share/_njEukei9D_JdhVufeZ2yjy9TiAsw5jD?tab=summary&timestamp=822.0)
      - [**Cross-functional Alignment:** Facilitated design thinking workshops to build consensus on health indicators and their weighting.](https://fathom.video/share/_njEukei9D_JdhVufeZ2yjy9TiAsw5jD?tab=summary&timestamp=822.0)
      - [**Executive Sponsorship:** Used the VP of CS to champion the project, tying it to company OKRs to drive buy-in.](https://fathom.video/share/_njEukei9D_JdhVufeZ2yjy9TiAsw5jD?tab=summary&timestamp=1103.0)
      - [**Process Improvement:** Created a formal "Strategic Initiatives Intake Process" to vet new projects by effort and impact, ensuring focus on top priorities.](https://fathom.video/share/_njEukei9D_JdhVufeZ2yjy9TiAsw5jD?tab=summary&timestamp=1192.0)
  - [**Collaboration with Data Team:**](https://fathom.video/share/_njEukei9D_JdhVufeZ2yjy9TiAsw5jD?tab=summary&timestamp=1394.0)
      - [**Process:** LeGrand provides UI/UX mockups (e.g., PowerPoint) to define requirements, leaving the technical implementation to the data team.](https://fathom.video/share/_njEukei9D_JdhVufeZ2yjy9TiAsw5jD?tab=summary&timestamp=1394.0)
      - [**Negotiation:** Balances development effort vs. user experience (e.g., chose Salesforce over Gainsight for a 5x faster build).](https://fathom.video/share/_njEukei9D_JdhVufeZ2yjy9TiAsw5jD?tab=summary&timestamp=1514.0)
      - [**Tools:** Gainsight, Tableau, Salesforce, Gong, Jira.](https://fathom.video/share/_njEukei9D_JdhVufeZ2yjy9TiAsw5jD?tab=summary&timestamp=1617.0)

## Next Steps

  - [**Bridget McGillivray:** Calibrate with Andy (hiring manager) on the role's scope, specifically regarding dashboard development capabilities.](https://fathom.video/share/_njEukei9D_JdhVufeZ2yjy9TiAsw5jD?tab=summary&timestamp=2163.0)
  - [**Bridget McGillivray:** Follow up with Emily LeGrand within a few days.](https://fathom.video/share/_njEukei9D_JdhVufeZ2yjy9TiAsw5jD?tab=summary&timestamp=2272.0)

action_items.description: Sync w/ Andy re: role scope + data/dashboard ownership; update Emily
